text: That was the real tragedy of this world; the Creator’s children destroying one another without thought . Amidst all that, had Ronin somehow found love ? Why else would he be so ready and willing to give up the existence he’d known for decades? He was ready to cast logic aside to pursue an emotion he shouldn’t ever have experienced . The Clinic was quiet as he turned off the main street and approached it. The gearheads on guard outside stared at him, making no comment as he passed them. Only the glass entry doors were illuminated. Inside, the place was as white and sanitized as ever, so unchanged that when Mercy — in her usual spot at the front desk — looked up at him, he wondered if he’d lapsed into a full-sensory memory. His stride was suddenly weightless, and everything moved too slowly . Mercy’s lips parted, but she didn’t smile, didn’t greet him. Following her gaze, he glanced down at his ruined torso . “Just need some reskinning,” he said . She didn’t seem to appreciate the humor . Chapter Twenty - Two R onin watched as the skin on his right leg was carefully cut and peeled away. The machine’s larger arms lowered, and numerous attachments went to work. Within a minute, everything below his right knee had been removed. He didn’t power off the sensors in the area. The pain was intense, but fleeting; a brief flicker of life. Such sensations were both more intense and longer-lasting for humans . After his leg was repaired and reattached, the machine moved upward, filling in the holes in his casing and making minor repairs to his damaged internals. Finally, it loomed over his head, tiny arms cutting away the shrapnel embedded in his left optic. Before repairs were made, it switched off his optical input . He occupied himself by sifting through his memory. He replayed Lara’s dance, reviewed their conversations and their arguments, lamented the amount of time he’d spent apart from her. Echoes of what he’d experienced during their numerous couplings skittered across his electrodes, lighting up skin that was no longer present . More than anything else, he replayed her confession of love . The attendant directed Ronin to the epidermal synthesizer once his optics were online. He continued his reveries in the chamber . He hadn’t told Lara he loved her. Hadn’t known how, though the words were so simple; it would have taken less than one and a half seconds of speech. She’d given no indication that she was bothered by it, but that didn’t make it all right . When the repairs were complete, he dressed — the shirt Lara made was more comfortable against his skin than anything had ever been, apart from Lara herself — and thanked the attendant. He’d been away from home for twelve hours and thirty-five minutes, and wanted nothing more than to be back with her. To tell her that he loved her , too . An itch pulsed across his cheek. He ignored it . “Good to see you fully repaired,” Mercy said pleasantly . Ronin glanced at her and halted abruptly. Her smile was gone, belying her tone . “What’s wrong?” he asked . “Nothing.” Her optics flicked to the entrance. “It’s just that… he came looking for you, while you were in there .” “He want me to go see him ?” “No.” She dropped her gaze and pressed her pink lips together. “He’s been waiting outside for the last three hours and forty-two minutes .” Ronin turned his head to the doors. Between the contrasting lighting and the reflections on the glass, the details were blurry, but he counted five distinct figures standing about thirty feet from the entrance . There were undoubtedly other exits from the building, but Ronin had only been in the lobby and the repair room. He dismissed the possibility. Such an escape would only lead Warlord to Ronin’s residence, and he didn’t want this scar-faced bot anywhere near Lara . He walked toward them, ceasing his processors’ attempted speculation — Warlord’s whims seemed as difficult to predict as Lara’s. The doors whirred open, their sound indicating an inevitable motor failure sometime in the future . If the group of bots had been conversing, their words fell silent when Ronin stepped outside. They all looked at him as he approached. Warlord stood at the center of the group, unassumingly average in height, build, and features compared to his companions, save for the sutured gash on his cheek. Even though the gearheads displayed far more bare metal, Warlord’s damage was somehow more imposing . Ronin stopped several feet away . Should have brought the pistol . A single round in Warlord’s optic might have been enough to short his system entirely . “Dustwalker. Looking better than you did when you came in last night,” Warlord said, expression neutral . “Feeling it, too.” Ronin met the optics of each of the gearheads before settling his focus on Warlord again. “Just here to make sure I’m repaired ?” “You push boundaries, dustwalker. Maybe that’s good, out there. Maybe that’s why you’re so productive. But we’ve been over this already .” “You know the condition I was in when I came back, and the sun was still up .” “I’ve dealt with your type. I understand you. I told you that my rules are to be obeyed, didn’t I? I don’t think my memory’s been corrupted in the last month. Maybe you can refresh me, so we can be sure .” “Your bots let me through without a word . They — ” “You know that’s not what this is about!” Warlord growled, jabbing a finger at Ronin and stepping forward. “You’re smarter than that, dustwalker. Or you think you are, anyway .” Ronin’s processors blazed through data, searching every moment he’d spent in Cheyenne. There weren’t many conversations with Warlord to go over, but there were always rules, rarely presented
